title: Notifier Configuration
description: Configuration file for the generic web scraper and email notifier. Defines SMTP settings, reusable scraping definitions, and rules that combine definitions with schedules, templates, and recipients. The config file is located at ~/.notifier/config.json.

$schema.description: Optional reference to this JSON Schema for editor validation.

server.description: SMTP server credentials used to send notification emails.

port.description: SMTP server port. Typically 587 for STARTTLS or 465 for SSL.

email.description: Sender email address. Also used as the SMTP login username.

password.description: SMTP login password or app-specific password.

defs.description: Reusable scraping definitions. Each key is a definition name referenced by rules via the 'ref' field. A definition describes how to fetch a URL, paginate, and extract structured data from HTML using CSS selectors. The optional 'commands' key defines reusable Liquid tag commands.

commands.description: Reusable Liquid tag commands. Each key becomes a custom {% tag %} usable in validator test/match expressions. Commands are templates rendered with positional arguments.

args.description: Positional argument names. Values are passed in order when the tag is used: {% cmd arg1 arg2 %}. 'word' args resolve as variables from item context, numeric literals are passed as numbers.

template.description: Liquid template string to render. Use argument names as variables: {{ field | date: "%s" }}.

^validators$.description: Reusable validator definitions. Each key is a validator name that can be referenced by rules via {"@id": "name"} in a validator field.

rules.description: List of rules to execute. Each rule references a definition and specifies scheduling, email template, recipient, and parameter values.

definition.description: A scraping definition that describes how to fetch and parse data from a website.

format.description: 'html': parse the response as HTML using the default html.parser (default). 'xml': parse the response as XML using the lxml XML parser. Use this for RSS/Atom feeds or any XML document where CSS selectors target XML elements.

userAgent.description: Custom User-Agent header for HTTP requests. If omitted, the default browser-like User-Agent is used. Useful for RSS feeds or APIs that require a specific User-Agent.

url.description: URL to fetch. Supports Liquid template variables from rule params, e.g. 'https://example.com/search?q={{query}}'. Variables are enclosed in double curly braces.

params.description: List of parameter names used in the URL template. These must be provided in each rule's 'params' object.

pagination.description: Controls how the scraper follows pagination to fetch multiple pages of results.

type.description: Pagination strategy. 'next_link': follow a single 'Next' or 'More' link (e.g. Hacker News). 'numbered': find the active page button and follow the next numbered page link (e.g. useme.com).

selector.description: CSS selector for the pagination element. For 'next_link': the 'next page' link (e.g. 'a.morelink'). For 'numbered': all page number links (e.g. '.pagination .pagination__page').

active_class.description: Only for type 'numbered'. CSS class name on the currently active page link (e.g. 'pagination__page--active'). Used to find the next page.

base_url.description: Base URL for resolving relative href values in pagination links.

max_pages.description: Maximum number of pages to fetch. Prevents unbounded crawling.

query.description: Defines how to extract structured data from the fetched HTML page.

type.description: 'list': select multiple elements (e.g. all job listings on a page). 'single': select one element (e.g. the latest blog post).

selector.description: CSS selector for the item container(s). For 'list', this matches multiple elements. For 'single', it matches the first element.

expect.description: List of CSS selectors that must exist on the page. If any are missing, the scraper sends an error email about HTML structure changes instead of silently failing. Checked on the first page only.

variables.description: Named variables to extract from each matched element. Each key becomes a variable name available in the email template (e.g. {{title}}, {{url}}).

idSpec.description: Defines how to extract a unique identifier for each item, used for deduplication between runs. Two strategies are available.

oneOf.description: Extract ID from a variable value using an optional regex. Example: extract '12345' from URL '/jobs/slug,12345/' using regex ',(\d+)/$'.

source.description: Name of a variable (from 'variables') or a param (from 'input'/'params') to use as the ID source. When using 'input', params are merged into items before ID extraction.

regex.description: Optional regex pattern to extract the ID from the source value. If the regex has a capture group, group(1) is used. Otherwise the full match is used.

oneOf.description: Read the ID directly from an HTML attribute on the matched element. Example: <tr id='47415919'> with name='id' gives '47415919'.

type.description: Must be 'attribute' to read an HTML attribute from the element.

name.description: HTML attribute name to read from the matched element.

filter.description: Exclude items that match a condition. The filter looks for a sub-element and skips the item if it has a specific CSS class.

selector.description: CSS selector for the element to check within each item.

exclude_class.description: If the selected element has this CSS class, the item is excluded.

variable.description: Defines how to extract a named value from a matched element using a CSS selector. By default extracts from the first matching element. Set 'collect' to true to extract from ALL matching elements and join them. When combined with parse 'json' and a query, extracts structured data from JSON content (e.g. Next.js __NEXT_DATA__).

selector.description: CSS selector relative to the item container (or its sibling if 'sibling' is true). Use the special value ':self' to reference the container element itself. For JSON extraction, use a global selector like 'script#__NEXT_DATA__'.

default.description: Fallback value if the selector doesn't match or the extracted value is empty.

sibling.description: When true, the selector is applied to the next sibling element of the item container instead of within it. Useful when data is split across adjacent HTML elements (e.g. Hacker News stores the title in one <tr> and the score in the next <tr>).

collect.description: When true, ALL elements matching the selector are collected (using select() instead of select_one()). Each value is extracted and returned as a list. Use {% for skill in item.skills %}...{% endfor %} in templates to iterate. Useful for extracting lists of tags, skills, or categories from repeated elements.

valueSpec.description: Specifies what to extract from the selected element.

type.description: 'text': extract the inner text content of the element (trimmed). 'attribute': extract the value of an HTML attribute.

name.description: Required when type is 'attribute'. The HTML attribute name to read.

regex.description: Optional regex applied to the extracted value. If the regex has a capture group, group(1) is used. Otherwise the full match is used. If the regex doesn't match, the default value is returned.

prefix.description: String prepended to the final value after regex extraction. Commonly used to turn relative URLs into absolute URLs.

parse.description: Parse the extracted string into a typed value. 'number': plain numeric parsing. 'money': locale-aware currency parsing via babel. 'list': split into a list using 'delimiter' regex. 'json': parse as JSON, then optionally query with JMESPath via the 'query' field. Useful for extracting structured data from <script> tags (e.g. Next.js __NEXT_DATA__) or JSON in HTML attributes.

delimiter.description: Regex pattern used to split the value when parse is 'list'. Defaults to '\s*,\s*' (comma with optional surrounding whitespace).

jsonQuery.description: Defines how to extract variables from parsed JSON using JMESPath. Used when parse is 'json'. Mirrors the HTML query structure with type (single/list), a JMESPath path, and named variables.

type.description: 'list': the JMESPath result is an array, extract variables from each entry. Returns a list of objects. 'single': extract variables from one result object. Returns a flat object.

path.description: JMESPath expression to navigate the parsed JSON. Supports Liquid variables (e.g. `{{id}}`) rendered against the current item's data before querying. If omitted, the root JSON object is used. See https://jmespath.org/ for syntax.

variables.description: Named variables to extract from each JSON result entry. Each key becomes accessible in templates (e.g. {{ loc.city }}).

jsonVariable.description: Defines how to extract a value from a JSON entry using a JMESPath sub-expression.

path.description: JMESPath expression to extract a value from the JSON entry. If omitted, the entry itself is used.

rule.description: A rule that combines a scraping definition with scheduling, email delivery, and template rendering. Each rule produces a separate email notification when new items are detected.

ref.description: Name of a definition in the 'defs' object to use for scraping.

name.description: Unique rule identifier. Used as the filename for state storage (~/.notifier/data/<name>) and last-run tracking. Must contain only letters, numbers, hyphens, and underscores.

schedule.description: Cron expression(s) controlling when this rule executes. A single string or an array of strings (any match triggers the rule). If omitted, the rule runs on every invocation.

subject.description: Liquid template for the email subject line. Available variables: {{count}} (number of new items), {{now}} (current datetime), {{search_url}} (the fetched URL), all rule params (e.g. {{query}}), and all extracted variables.

template.description: Path to the Liquid template file for the email body, relative to ~/.notifier/. The template has access to: {{count}}, {{now}}, {{search_url}}, {% for item in items %}...{% endfor %} loop with {{ item.index }} and all extracted variables, plus all rule params.

email.description: Recipient email address for notifications from this rule.

params.description: Parameter values for the definition's URL template. Keys must match the definition's 'params' list. Values are also available in email templates. Used when 'input' is not specified.

input.description: One or more input entries, each with its own params and optional validator. Use this to scrape multiple pages of the same type (e.g. multiple stock symbols) into a single rule. Can be a single object or an array. If omitted, 'params' is used directly.

inputEntry.description: A single input entry that provides parameters for one page fetch and an optional validator to filter results.

params.description: Parameter values for this input's URL. Overrides the rule-level 'params'. Also merged into extracted items so they're available in templates.

validator.description: Filter extracted items. A single validator object (AND logic within), an array of validators (OR logic — item passes if any validator passes), or a reference to a reusable validator via {"@id": "name"}. In an array, validators with 'require: true' must ALL pass; at least one non-required validator must also pass. Array elements can also be @id references.

validatorRef.description: A reference to a reusable validator defined in defs.validators. The @id value must match a key in defs.validators.

@id.description: The name of the validator in defs.validators to use.

validator.description: Validates extracted items. Each validator must contain exactly one condition type: either 'test' or 'match', not both. In an array of validators, those with 'require: true' must ALL pass (AND logic), and at least one non-required validator must also pass (OR logic).

require.description: When true, this validator must always pass (AND logic with other validators). Non-required validators in the same array use OR logic. Use this for baseline filters (e.g. minimum score) combined with threshold alerts.

test.description: A numexpr expression with Liquid variable placeholders. Variables with 'parse: number' are available as floats. The expression must evaluate to true for the item to be included. Use & for AND, | for OR, and parentheses for grouping. Liquid filters are available, e.g. {{ date | date: "%Y%m%d" }} or {{ "now" | date: "%u" }}.

match.description: Regex match condition(s). A single match object or an array of match objects (AND logic — all must pass).

matchCondition.description: A match condition against a variable value. Use 'value' for Liquid-rendered strings or 'var' for raw variable lookup (preserves lists). Condition: 'regex' for pattern matching, 'include' for list membership, or 'exclude' for list exclusion.

value.description: Liquid template string to render against item variables. The rendered result is tested against the condition.

var.description: Direct variable name lookup — returns the raw value (preserves lists from collect: true). Use instead of 'value' when you need exact element matching on list variables.

regex.description: Regex pattern to match against the value. Uses re.search() so it matches anywhere in the string unless anchored with ^ or $. For list values, elements are joined with ', ' before matching.

include.description: List of strings — passes if any string is found. For list values (via 'var'), checks exact element membership. For strings, checks substring match (or exact match with 'strict: true').

exclude.description: List of strings — passes if none are found. For list values (via 'var'), checks exact element membership. For strings, checks substring match (or exact match with 'strict: true').

strict.description: When true, include/exclude use exact string equality instead of substring match. Only affects string values — list values always use exact element matching. Default is false.

exist.description: Whether the regex pattern should exist in the value. Set to false to detect when something disappears from a page. Default is true (pattern must match). Not needed with 'exclude' which is already inverted.
